metric_value: Single consistent FRAD product line for enterprise data interconnect; TurboFRAD (branch), OmniFRAD (hub), NetFRAD (carrier); Matrix VC switching architecture
RFC 1490 FRAD: 9 bytes/frame; TCP/IP SDLC encapsulation (router): 70 bytes; DLS (router): 50 bytes. Routers consume 50%+ of SNA frame at peak loading.
Payback in 12-18 months; some customers in less than 12 months; driven by 20-25% reduction in leased-line costs plus MIS labor and network licensing savings

Rapidly growing service since 1991 offerings; IXCs dominate segment; RBOCs gaining foothold; frame relay eroding portion of leased-lines market
Aberdeen predicts ATM will appear first as backbone transmission fed by frame relay 'edge network'; frame relay will NOT be displaced by ATM

metric_name: Frame Relay / ATM Migration Actual Outcome
metric_value: ATM was deployed as backbone in telco networks while frame relay persisted as edge access through early 2000s; both eventually superseded by MPLS/IP, not one replacing the other as Aberdeen predicted
notes: Prediction directionally correct: ATM did appear as backbone while frame relay persisted as edge

metric_value: Aberdeen believes Netlink FRADs represent a sound investment decision with robust data services market continuing into next century; Matrix VC provides smooth transition to future capabilities

Netlink acquired by Cabletron Systems for ~$158-160M in stock in September 1996 — same month as this profile; Cabletron subsequently dissolved into 4 entities by 2001; frame relay products eventually discontinued
Only FRADs using all available bandwidth of frame relay line; local SNA acknowledgment reduces WAN load; transparent backup reroutes around network failures

metric_name: FRAD vs Router for SNA Environments
metric_value: Enterprises with SNA traffic >40% or performance-sensitive SNA will benefit greatly from FRADs over routers; routers far less efficient at SNA traffic handling
Frame relay evolved from X.25 digital packet switching; eliminated X.25 error correction/flow overhead in network layer; error correction delegated to end-user device (FRAD/router)
Private/hybrid frame relay edge switch with backbone switch capabilities plus multiprotocol FRAD and routing functions; SNMP-managed via OmniView on HP OpenView
IBM and Netlink have nationwide service agreement enabling quick response for SNA network customers; Anixter as global SI partner

metric_name: ATM Competitive Position vs Frame Relay
metric_value: Cell-relay ATM for high-throughput T3+ real-time apps being developed to integrate with frame relay base; ATM targets integrated voice/video/data
